---
title: 'What can you do, to not allow yourself to fall "in love" too-far too-fast? There is an answer.'
date: '2020-04-01T10:49:00.0000000+00:00'
draft: true
blogger_blog: 'Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller'
blogger_status: 'LIVE'
blogger_post_id: '1639572729807280960'
blogger_filename: '/2020/04/what-can-you-do-to-not-allow-yourself.html'
blogger_source: 'takeout-20260223T235336Z-3-001.zip:Takeout/Blogger/Blogs/Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller/feed.atom'
categories:
  - 'Musings'
---

<div><b>You CAN prevent yourself from "falling" for someone. It's within your power. In fact, you <u>will</u> if you want to be happy and healthy.</b></div><div><br></div><div>Every so often... I see a question that needs to be written about. In this case, maybe a book written about it. This is one of those. I've removed all private details, and boiled it down to the very question, word for word, I asked my Counselor in July 2019, in order to understand how I'd allowed myself to get so hurt so badly from someone I barely knew.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><b>Question</b>:</div><div><br></div><div><i>How can you slow down the speed and momentum of the relationship?&nbsp;</i></div><div><i><br></i></div><div><i>What can you do, to not allow yourself to fall "in love" too-far too-fast?</i></div><div><i><br></i></div><div><i>Can you help "falling in love"? Isn't that beyond your control? After</i><i>&nbsp;all, the heart will do what the heart will do?</i></div><div><br></div><div><b>Answer</b>:</div><div><div><br></div><div>Intimacy, Connection, and feeling "in love" is <b><i><u>not</u></i></b> accidental or without cause.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><b><i><u>You don't "fall" in love. You walk into it, one mini-action at a time.&nbsp;</u></i></b></div><div><br></div><div>Dr Henry Cloud wrote about this in one of his dating books, I read two of his.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div>*<a href="https://amzn.to/2Obr9AM">How to get a date worth keeping</a>. <i>Affiliate</i></div><div>*<a href="https://amzn.to/2QuhR5U">Boundaries in dating</a>. <i>Affiliate</i></div><div><br></div><div>When you text all day, everyday, spend hours on the phone, ramp up physical connection through kissing, touch, or more, and send messages like "Hey, just thinking about you"...&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div>Each of these actions builds connection.</div><div><br></div><div>In and of themselves, that's wonderful. <i>If</i> they're appropriate for the stage of relationship. If you've built something over time and it's appropriate.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div>Six weeks, not so much.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div>Six months, probably.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div>Married six years, you'd better be doing these!&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><i><b>Side Note</b>: This works when used on purpose too! If you're already married, doing these intentionally will help build and maintain the "in love" factor long into your elderly years.</i></div><div><br></div><div>However, early in a relationship, if you're loosing yourself, ignoring other priorities, or feeling it's going "too fast"... Then these are red flags.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div>Stop or slow down the activities that built the sudden ramp-up of connection. Set a boundary on your time and emotional health.</div><div><br></div><div>Since this would be a change, if you didn't do this from the beginning, the other person deserves to know something is changing.</div><div><br></div><div>Good boundary statements focus on your needs and not the other person's side.</div><div><br></div><div>Keep it to 'I need' statements. Use your own words but consider something like the following:</div><div><br></div><div>"I want you to know I REALLY like you. I am also afraid I'm loosing myself in this and I need to protect my heart and yours. So I need to slow down. As such, I won't be texting all day every day, or spending all my time on the phone with you. I have other priorities that are going unfinished because I've been so excited. If you'll stick with me, I'd like to build something, just slower. That also means, I won't be kissing you again for a little while, just until I feel we've really reached the level of commitment that makes my heart safe."<br></div><div><br></div><div>Followed by:</div><div><br></div><div>"I want you to hear my heart. Do you understand what I'm saying?"<br></div><div><br></div><div>"Tell me how your feeling about that?"<br></div><div><br></div><div><b><i><u>Okay. Now let's break that down:</u></i></b></div><div><br></div><div><b>Affirm the relationship</b>: I want you to know I REALLY like you.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><b>Set up the concern</b>: I am also afraid I'm loosing myself in this and I need to protect my heart and yours. So I need to slow down.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><b>Set the Boundary</b>: As such, I won't be (<i>insert whatever behavior needs to change</i>) texting&nbsp;all day every day, or spending all my time on the phone with you. I have other priorities that are going unfinished <u>because I've been so excited</u> (<i>there's more affirmation baked in</i>).</div><div><br></div><div><b>Reaffirm the relationship</b>: If you'll stick with me, I'd like to build something, just slower.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><b>Reaffirm the boundary</b>: That also means, I won't be kissing you again for a little while, just until I feel we've really reached the level of commitment that makes my heart safe.</div><div><br></div><div><b>Check for understanding</b>: I want you to hear my heart. Do you understand what I'm saying?</div><div><br></div><div><b>Check for any misunderstanding or hurt</b>: Tell me how your feeling about that?&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div>Something like that. These are just guidelines. Not exact statement rules. Just to give you ideas.</div><div><br></div><div><br></div><div><b>Possible outcomes and what they mean</b>:</div><div><br></div><div>* If you receive total understanding and compliance, you've got a great person! Very healthy.</div><div><br></div><div>* If you receive hurt or confusion from their side, that's emotionally immature but normal. Maybe both commit to read some books on Boundaries together instead of whatever you were doing.</div><div><br></div><div>* If you receive anything strongly negative, anger, frustration, passive aggressiveness, lashing out, begging, etc.... You have a seriously Boundary Injured individual <i>(I should know. I was such an individual until I got healing. I've also dated a few</i>).</div><div><br></div><div>If you have this third reaction, that's still not an automatic termination of relationship, but, it's a STRONG red flag. Insist on reading books on Boundaries together, Podcasts, classes, get a counselor if you really feel the relational is worth fighting for. But do not take any further steps to commitment until this behavior is <i>firmly</i> behind you. If you were ring shopping, stop. If you are engaged, postpone. If you already sent out invitations to the wedding (<i>you are probably beyond this post if that's true, but, if you feel this applies</i>), send out postponement letters.</div><div><br></div><div><b>Get a team! Before, During, and After.</b></div><div><br></div><div><b>Get friends involved</b>: Also, have a friend (or five) you can report everything to on a regular basis. People who know you well. People who will call you out when you're not being yourself or being authentic or being the best you.</div><div><br></div><div><b>Professional assistance</b>: I think everyone on Earth should have a qualified licensed mental health counselor they see regularly. At least as regularly it more as they would a primary care physician or dentist. A good pastor or mentor or elder brother or sister (<i>who is qualified to coach or counsel</i>) is a good idea too. Qualified doesn't mean licensed. It just means that they demonstrate the maturity and capacity and emotional health to coach you. They should exhibit a lifestyle and brand of living you want to have in your life. If you have a power hungry leader, as I've had a few times, these do not make good coaches. They're more likely to hurt you then help.&nbsp;</div><div><br></div><div><i><b>CAUTION</b>: Not all pastor's, coaches, or even licensed counselors are created equal, do your due diligence to vet out the options until you find someone that can really help you</i>. <i>This will be someone with a shared worldview, or a worldview you admire, who you trust and who shows the capacity to emphasize, build your confidence, but still challenge your thinking</i>.</div><div><br></div><div>There's probably more I could add. I'm sure I will in another post. But that's enough to chew on for now.</div><div><br></div><div>Live long and prosper! Shalom!</div><div><br></div><div>#Selah</div><div><br></div><div>Darrell&nbsp;</div><div><br></div></div><div><div class="separator" style="clear: both; text-align: center;">
  <a href="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/1585743416028181-0-7f825b7d5426.png" imageanchor="1" style="margin-left: 1em; margin-right: 1em;">
    <img border="0"   src="/images/imported/blogger-googleusercontent-com/1585743416028181-0-7f825b7d5426.png" width="400">
  </a>
</div><br></div>
